Hi Everyone -
I thought I would contribute to the collective knowledge here with my journey busting using Rivea Corymbosa Seeds, AKA "RC Seeds", AKA Ololiuqui. I will keep a log of it all in the format I've shown below. First I will give some quick background. (To skip to just the busting log, scroll to the bottom).
I'm a 35 year old male, first started getting CH when I was sixteen, in 2001. Cycles are generally ~7 weeks, and have been getting further and further apart the older I get, with ~3 years between cycles. When I was a teen I did the pharma route, finding mixed results with Prednisone taper and Inderal. But once those stopped working, a neurologist with a bad attitude started trying other drugs on me, and I started reading about the experiences of other CHers on clusterheadaches.com and here on clusterbusters, I said "no thank you" to the pharma route.
2003 - 2010
Sloppily experimenting with busting, different vitamins, prednisone/Inderal etc. I was 18-25 during this time, and you know how people are at that age! I had mixed results with mushrooms, and also had very mixed results with the high! One of the most enjoyable nights in my entire life was on mushrooms, and it stopped the CH dead in its tracks. I also had some of the most intensely anxious times in my life, and it didn't stop the CH. I attempted RC Seeds during this time, but back then the recommended starting dose was very low. I think I started with 8 seeds! So, I found no impact from using them.
2010 - 2020
Started the Vitamin D3 regimen. The D3 brought the severity of the pain during attacks WAY down. I've never been able to fully control a cycle using just the D3 regimen, but it has helped tremendously. During this time I also started using oxygen - *properly*! I was prescribed oxygen as a teenager, but was using the wrong mask and didn't have enough air flow, so I concluded that it didn't work for me. This was a total game-changer, obviously. I also started realizing that when I eat, I get attacks. I started talking to Batch a lot during this time. We talked about the body's PH and how keeping the body more alkaline could help. So I started drinking more lemon water, making tweaks like that, that I think helped a little bit. I tried 5-MEO-DALT for two cycles during this time as well. The results were mixed and I did not like the idea of weighing out these tiny, tiny increments of a random chemical I purchased in a sketchy way on the internet and ingesting it.
Latest Cycle - Started Nov. 2020
This cycle has been different for a number of reasons: 1) It came faster than expected. Typically the pain free time between my cycles increases by 3 months each cycle. It is crazy how accurately it has followed it my entire life! 3 months between my first cycle and my second, then six months, then 9 months, etc. etc. I was all the way up to three years between cycles, and then this latest one came after 2.5 years. This was very disappointing for me. 2) It has lasted much longer than

Last night, completed Bust 4 with a 1/2 tab of Vitamin L.  Have not had a legit attack since before Bust 3 but still experience soreness and occasional shadows, so I thought one more Bust would be appropriate.
On the Bust itself:
- significant body discomfort, experienced mostly as jaw and neck tension combined with agitation.  This was not a serious problem, but it was not especially pleasant either.  I paced quite a bit
- lots of visual changes in the form of shifting patterns and breathing floors, walls, etc
All in all,
though I have certainly had some good times, I am
ready for a break from The Vitamin L recreational experience.
medicinal effects:
- during the Bust, experienced occasional pressure in CH area.  This was not significant enough to qualify as a shadow.
That's about it!
The night before last, I went out to a nice dinner with my SO and another couple we've had planned for months.  We had a wine pairing with our dinner, so I had seven different wines, mostly reds, and a sweet wine with dessert.  I mention it because red wine is an almost guaranteed trigger for me as is anything overly sweet.  The meal passed without even a shadow!  I even had a couple martinis at cocktail hour before dinner.  I had an injection of Imitrex back in my room in case things broke bad, but did not have to think about it.
normally, I wouldn't dream of drinking so much as a beer during or around a CH cycle but with the success of the Vitamin L and D, I was able to enjoy myself.  It's a different world.  We've spent the past month wondering if we should cancel this dinner because of my cycle; quite empowering to go through with it like this.
*****
if anybody is reading this looking for advice:
-start the MM or L as soon as you can, and space them apart 5 days, per protocol.  I did 4 to avoid a Christmas Day Bust but I think 5 would have been better
-start the Vitamin D regime immediately. I eventually doubled my D to 20,000 ius a day but YMMV.  I'm a big guy.
-try aborting attacks with ice cold water and 5 hour energy drinks (these are far superior to any others, because you can bolt them down immediately).
-very strong ginger for shadows and neck massages at cluster spots, specifically, tendons on side of neck and under ear.  Strong coffee helped my shadows as well
-only take Imitrex if it's an emergency.  I have found I get significant increase in cluster attacks following Imitrex use and a marked increase in pain level
-keep a diary and be methodical.   When did your cycle start?  When does each attack start? How long does an attack last?  What did you do to stop it?  Use this to identify possible triggers and possible actions that help you.   It's also something to focus on and be constructive with when you're up in the middle of the night in excruciating pain
-find a doctor who will prescribe oxygen for you. It is not expensive out of pocket or through insurance.  Advocate for yourself on this or ask someone to help you advocate.

Hello Cluster Family,
I just wanted to share my current experience with you all. First, a little back story, I have been a sufferer of clusters since I was 15 years old, I have seen so many different doctors and been given more pills than I could ever count. None of it helped. I had a cluster 1 week after giving birth to my youngest son, and all I remember was sitting on the bathroom floor, sleep deprived, in agony, legitimately wondering if I was going to lose my grip on reality and go insane.
Earlier this year I finally saw a neurologist that officially diagnosed me with CH, and I remember I immediately felt relief, because I had thought for years that this is what I had, but no doctor would diagnose me because the disorder is "primarily in men". It only took seconds for the relief to suddenly be taken over by despair. An incurable neurological disorder, that there is no known cause or cure. I spent several months in severe depression and anxiety, accompanied by insomnia from fear of sleep, as that is when my attack occur.
My clusters are normally triggered by extreme stress, or seasonal changes, so when summer started giving way to fall, my normal 6 week cluster period started. For 3 weeks I was going through 2 oxygen tanks a week, being woken up hourly from midnight to 7 am in excruciating pain. After being shamed into taking more prescription meds by my neurologist, I took verapamil for the first time. I took it at 5pm, and by 730 I was in the worst pain I have ever been in. I know this cluster was brought on by the medication because I do not normally get attacks in the evening. My clusters are very predictable, and always occur at night once I have gone to sleep. This attack very nearly put me in the hospital, and the only reason it didn't was because my husband gave me something that calmed me down enough to be able to withstand the pain.
The following day, I posted my first post on this page, asking for any "alternative" remedies that others had found helpful. I was put in contact with Batch, who gave me information on the Vitamin D3 regimen. (Which I have linked below)
https://vitamindwiki.com/Cluster+headaches+substantially+reduced+by+10,000+IU+of+Vitamin+D+in+80+percent+of+people
Unfortunately, I did not have all the items in the regimen in my medicine cabinet, but I live in the Pacific North West, and as we do get less sun here than in other parts of the county, so I had previously purchased a  vitamin D3 dropper with 5,000ui per serving, which I had not taken because I am habitually bad at remembering to take medication or vitamins. So I decided to start taking two doses in the morning. I figured it couldn't hurt.
Now remember, at this time I am at about the halfway point in my normal 6 week cluster period. I started taking the vitamin D3, and did not feel any different the first night. However, the second night I was only woken up by about 3 clusters, all of which were about 50% less painful than normal. The following night, I was

First post but have been reading the board for years. I wanted to share what has been working for me for the past year and a half. Usual caveats- not a doctor, your mileage may vary, and this is just my experience.
For some background, I have had cluster headaches for going on 12 years. Every year has a minimum of two episodes that last 8-10 weeks. Each episode will have typically 2-3 headaches per day. Emgality and verapamil didn’t prevent or stop clusters for me. I have had strong side effects from medicines like sumatriptan and Topamax and refused to use them any longer.
I tried the clusterbuster program and had mild success on my on days, but immediately fell back into headaches on the days off with no lasting relief.
Last year I tried the below supplement routine and have used it for my last three cycles. I have had shadows on the program but have been headache free for 18 months so far. It’s based on research showing low allopregnanolone levels in cluster headache patients and theorizing that the low levels are a part of the headache process
Treatment Plan:
I start at first sign of headaches or shadows.
Reducing the Severity of Headaches and Shadows:
aka preventing the trigeminal nerve from getting angry
Cetirizine:
10mg twice daily (h1 receptor antagonist. Low potential to cross blood brain barrier however some research shows it will slightly. Chosen over benedryl due to lack of sedation and cognitive impairment)
Famotidine:
20mg twice daily (h2 receptor antagonist, crosses blood brain barrier)
Quercetin:
500mg twice daily. (reduced global histamine response)
Raising Allopregnanolone levels and improving HTPA function:
to help fill in the gaps where the body is currently failing in production
Vitamin D3 with K2:
10,000mg of Vitamin D3 with 300mg of K2, taken once daily.
Pregnenolone:
50mg once daily to convert to allopregnanolone. Research shows pregnenolone readily converts to allopregnanolone. This can be titrated up as needed to reduce headache frequency and severity. I have gone up to 300mg during a cycle, and there is some research showing that it is well tolerated up to 500mg per day.
DHEA:
50mg daily to encourage the conversion of pregnenolone to allopregnanolone so it doesn’t convert down the testosterone pathway.
Ashwagandha:
250mg sensoril for reduced cortisol
Abortive:
At the first sign of a headache
Caffeine:
from coffee or tea. I avoid caffeine sources such as energy drinks or supplements containing niacin or other vasodilators, which is counter productive since we want the vasoconstriction of caffeine
Every 5 days step down pregnenolone dosage. If shadows return, step back up and repeat.
With this I have been able to keep my headaches away and combat shadows with a cup of coffee.
This is my third cycle using this program and I waited to post it until I had repeat success. I understand that this could be unique to me, however the repeated success I’ve had has made me want to share this protocol in case it can help anyone else.

Couldn't be more relieved to be writing this post, but I'm delighted to report that I'm 9 days pain free, so I am feeling quite confident that the beast is back in the box for the foreseeable. I wasn't able to find a huge number of detailed bust reports for LSD, so I wanted to write up a report that was quite thorough, in case it's useful to someone (even if it's just future me!)
Context: I'm 27, male, based in London. This is my third cycle so far, so I'm still very new to ECH. My first two cycles were two years apart. In my first cycle, I received a diagnosis after 6 weeks, found Custerbusters after 8 weeks, and spent the following 2 weeks on a slightly manic LSD binge, after being quite desperate for some relief. The cycle ended after that, unsurprisingly, though I was never sure if it was the L or the cycle ending naturally.
After two years pain free, it returned in 2017. In that cycle, I only had access to MM. It took 5 weeks to break it, but got there in the end. I then had 2 and a half years pain free before my current cycle, which followed a missed maintenance dose and an errant glass of wine in June.
Day 1: My cycle with a relatively manageable K5. I went for a relatively modest ~40ug LSD, mostly sub-hallucinogenic, in accordance with the regimen as it stood in 2015. I experienced mild slapbacks (a pressure building in my forehead) as the effects wore off. In the days that followed, my attacks remained consistent in terms of pain, with frequency staying at/below 1 per day.
Day 5: I got hit with a K8, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. I dosed again afterwards, around ~50ug. When I came down, I was hit with a K8 slapback, and then a K9 the following day. Frequency was now up to around 2-3/day, often with one attack significantly worse than others.
Day 9: Dosed again, this time ~100ug, after coming across more up to date advice which suggested 'recreational doses' for busting with L. I decided to bring the bust forward a day, basically because I had to be functional the next day, but also because in my recreational use I'd found that my tolerance level was usually unnoticeable after 3 days off. Perhaps this was a bad call though, as I got hit with 2 and a half hours of K10, hands down the worst attack of my life. Decided after this that I needed to get hold of some O2, as the caffeine and hot showers weren't particularly helpful anymore.
Day 12: Finally was able to start the D3 regimen. Also started cutting chocolate from my diet and stopped skipping meals, as I began to suspect these were triggers.
Day 16: After several days of battling my doctor for it, I finally got my hands on some O2. Can't believe I went without it for the past 16 days, and the two cycles before this one! I felt giddy with relief after killing my first attack with it, made a mental note to ensure I had it from day 1 of my next cycle.
Day 17: Dosed again with around~100ug LSD, very slapback heavy trip but super manageable with my tank by my side.
Days 17-22: Pain lev

Hi all,
Sorry about the long hiatus, I’m still alive and fighting the daily CCH battle. I figured I’m over due for an update.
ive been using Ketamine since last October w mixed results. When I first started it I was at my wits end dealing w CH.  6-12 attacks a day lasting on average 3 hrs if I didn’t do anything. Mixed results w Oxygen and trex makes my CH worse over time and isn’t really an option when you’re getting 200-300 attacks a month. Vitamin D helped initially but it will only stop my CH if I stay in the 180-250+ range, aka not safe. Batch tried his hardest to tailor the program to no real success.  I had no life and my parents insisted I go see a doc again. I was working w a Neuro who was several states away and he started ketamine once at 50-75mg/ml nasal spray giving me a max of 45ml a month. I had to use so much to get relief and the doc didn’t want to change my script due to my psych hx. So I gave up.  Months later my parents insisted I go to my previous pain doc who had tried months of SPG and occipital nerve blocks to no success. I told him about the 1 study that included CH Pts w CCH and some other HA conditions.  The study did many ketamine infusions then started Pts on nasal.  He decided to give me oral troches and nasal.  I was taking about a gram of ketamine a day total.  After a few days my CH got much better.  The ketamine would abort the attacks and my frequency and intensity dropped dramatically. Woo hoo I had a life again. Doc was not happy how much ketamine I was on and didn’t realize this was going to be my long term plan so he started to back down the ketamine and switched me to just oral.  I’m currently in 600mg of ketamine a day and my CH is better but my QOL still sucks.  Wake up all night w alarm clock CHs and it takes 2 (100mg troches) to stop a CH plus minus oxygen for the 8-10s.   My attacks last 15-45 min now instead of 3hrs. But I’m still getting 4-8 attacks a day.  If I use my ketamine too fast they come back w a vengeance in 3-5 days. I’ve asked to switch back to nasal only being that it is more effective but he is concerned about my psych hx despite my psych saying he doesn’t think the ketamine was the cause of a hospitalization but was my PTSD.
Im about to move to FL and will be without a doc for the foreseeable future and am concerned about not having ketamine or oxygen. On one hand the ketamine makes life bearable (may be an understatement considering how bad my CH was before) but it’s not stopping them all together as it did in the beginning at higher doses. The other thing is it’s very expensive $450/ month for 180troches. Still less expensive than trex injections but when you don’t have income and have to get help from your parents (who are not wealthy and in fixed income) eventually I’ll have to give it up unless I get disability.
in summation at higher doses 1g a day ketamine almost stopped my attacks dead n their tracks. But when I backed down to about half that dose I get hit 4-6 times a day kipp

HI all,
Long time viewer here, but first time posting.
I have had CH since 21, I'm 33 now and was diagnosed just last year.
Over the years I've seen dentists, optometrists, an ENT specialist, an allergist It wasnt until I visited the emergency room during a cycle in 2016 that a doctor gave me oxygen (attack disappeared) and told me "you have cluster headaches". Finally saw a neurologist who diagnosed me. I've had  the MRI and CT scan the whole jazz.
my cycles generally happen around daylight savings in March or around the first day of Spring, however the last 2 cycles I have had (2018 and 2019) were started from sudden weather changes.
In 2018 it was a day in January where we saw the temperature shoot up to 15 degrees Celsius out of nowhere, and this July it was when the temperature dropped to 15 degrees Celsius out of nowhere.
My 2018 cycle was the longest i have ever had at 10 weeks, usually they stick around for 5-6 weeks. I am always afraid that 10 weeks will become the new trend.
My neurologist put me on Topamax this year to which i saw small relief, it did bring them down from 4 a day to 2 a day. I did some reading and found that changing my diet to sugar free carb free while on Topamax gave me complete pain free status. I could even smoke cannabis which is usually a huge trigger for me while in cycle. The only problem with this was, I dropped 20-25 lbs since July 13. It was the first time I could see physically how this disease has affected me and it brought me to very dark places mentally.
Last tuesday I decided to give busting a try, I dont have any reservations towards psychedelics I just havent done any of them since highschool and was nervous. So I came off the topamax on Tuesday and waited the 5 days. I started the D3 regimen and was getting attacks daily, aborted by energy drinks.  On sunday I dropped a gram of MM and boy was it ever intense.Felt like I was putting my brain in a very tight high ponytail LOL. On Monday morning I received a visit from the beast. Yesterday.....nothing....smoked a dooby.....nothing. This Morning nothing. although I will dose again this Friday I truelly believe I have broken the cycle and I cannot say how elated I am.
I want to extend my thanks to this forum ...although i am always skeptic I believe I will continue with the D3 regimen and busting going forward.

21 year Episodic. Clusters used to happen every 9 months, lasted 1 month with 1 or two attack / day lasting 1 hour each. The cycle would be like clockwork, almost to the day. Back then I was treating with maxalt wafers only, and running cold water on my head. Eventually was prescribed amitriptyline, which unsurprisingly didn't do anything. Sometimes my cycle would
end
with a day-long attack. Different from a regular cluster headache, but, still on the cluster side. Really weird. Anyway, I would often end up in the ER with these ending attacks, but, they were always bad news / good news. Bad because I knew they would last at least 24 hours; and were brutal (not as bad as a CH attack, but, pretty unbearable due to the length of the attack and no sleep). Good because I knew the cycle would end after that one weird one. Eventually saw my first neurologist in 2012 after a trip to the ER (10 years after my CHs started). I've had all of the required brain scans, etc. and got the official diagnosis. Neurologist recommended maxalt wafers, home oxygen, prednisone taper at the start of a cycle, and verapamil for the duration of the cycle and for 6 weeks after it clears. Family doctor wouldn't prescribe me any of those things except for Maxalt. Said everything else was too risky.
Found a new Family Doctor 2 years later (2014). Got me hooked up with another Neurologist, and got the same treatment plan recommended. Got all of my scripts filled, etc.
Over the years, my cycles spread out longer (longer remission), but, more attacks while in cycle. One cycle, I didn't realize the risks of too many triptans (was taking 2, 3, sometimes 4 per day that cycle). Ended up getting really bad rebound headaches. Cycle lasted much longer, and I ended up getting upwards of 8 attacks per day. After that cycle, and realizing what had happened; I've tried to avoid the triptans whenever possible. I rely on home o2, and only use a maxalt nasal spray if I'm out and don't have access to o2 or any other options.
Started the D3 regimen in 2019, and have been taking all of the cofactors and everything daily since then.
For the last few cycles, my treatment plan has been:
1) 13 day prednisone taper at the onset of a cycle; 60mg 2 days, 50mg 3 days, 40mg 3 days, 30mg 2 days, 20mg 2 days, 10mg 1 day
2) Verapamil - 120mg x 3 / day
3) Home o2 - 25lpm through Cluster o2 mask (coupled with the odd sip of really cold ice water through a straw)
4) Rizatriptan Nasal spray only for emergencies (5mg)
5) Still doing the D3 regimen
6) Have tried adding melatonin in before bed (10mg)
7) Energy drinks to try to help clear shadows
8) Ice-pack on the head for shadows, and sometimes during attacks (other times I can't stand it)
9) No alcohol
The prednisone taper has killed 2 cycles right at the start. One cycle it didn't completely kill it (the cycle above with the triptan overuse); and I ended up doing a 2nd prednisone taper, which did end it
CURRENT CYCLE - started JUNE 17th

Hello everyone, I am sorry I haven’t responded until now but I do want to answer all of your questions. First, let me tell you a little more background about me. I have a degree in Nursing and worked as a Trauma/ER nurse and a Cardiac Catheterization Nurse for over 20 years. When Cluster Headaches came into my life I was a healthy adult with no prior history of any neurological disorders. I was diagnosed and went through all of the testing one would expect, MRI, Labwork etc. everything came back normal. I immediately started trying to find a “cure” for what was the worst pain and subsequent ancillary symptoms, I had ever experienced. I was familiar with the medical system and had many doctors try and help me. I have tried and had some short relief with therapies such as oxygen therapy, medication, Vitamin D therapy and micro-dosing Magic Mushrooms, high dose Botox injections, Lidocaine injections, Hot pepper nasal spray to name most of them. The problem that continued to occur was that I would get some short term relief, only to see the therapy slowly stop working and would go back into a full cycle.
i have had cycles that lasted 7 months and would have 4-8 severe attacks daily with many ancillary symptoms. I was literally barely surviving each horrific day. I was actively suicidal and honestly believed that I would eventually have to end my life to find freedom from the torment.
I found Ketamine and the clinic because I never stopped looking for something to help stop the attacks. I came across an article about Ketamine treatments for Migraines and they mentioned CH in the article as a possible subset that Ketamine could treat.  The Doctor I see has had over 15 years working with Ketamine and treating Depression and  all forms of headache disorders. We often discuss his observations and let me tell you, what he has seen bears shouting from the rooftops. He said to me during my last booster infusion, that he has seen better results treating CH than he does treating Migraines and his observations estimates about a 70-80% effective treatment with remission and with many with long term remissions at that.
As a Nurse, I have looked at CH as both a horrible and completely debilitating diagnosis for me, but also, with the eyes of a medical professional. I have tried to understand what could be an answer as to why this disorder is so under treated and resistant to so many therapies. I believe in my heart that I am not unique and that Ketamine could be a first line therapy that actually has a significant chance at putting many CH sufferers into long term remission. I decided to post about my experience because I have not seen much online or on any medical journals about Ketamine for CH and I cannot understand why this seems like a hidden treatment or a “last line” treatment. It should not be a treatment for those a the end of their rope, as I was. We all know why this is and if you have experienced any of what I have experienced as a patient seeking help

I have battled CH on and off for 13 years
it was only this past year that I realized cluster headaches are in fact a real thing and are in fact what's wrong with me. I have been trying to help myself for so long. treating with every kind of over the counter medication you can think of. always with no success. no real reliable relief. finally this year during my spring cycle things got so bad I wound up in the ER. more than once.  I never would have imagined clusters could do that to me.
that's what it took for me to realize what this was. all my life I thought I was sort of over reacting or being weak about something normal people go through with little trouble. as a kid in school I just had to put my head down and bare it. occasionally I would get some time in the clinic or sent home early. growing up it has always caused trouble with work. I don't like to look like a complainer or weak or someone who makes excuses. so I have always kept my mouth shut and soldiered through what I had to. and when I couldn't bare it, quietly submit to the pain at home before passing out on over the counter pain meds. anything to stop the pain.
I have finally come to terms with living with ch. with that I hope I can be of some help to others who may need it
There are 3 key things I have found to help me through an attack or a cycle
STAY CALM!
I have found that panic can make the pain more severe and during a more severe attack, especially if a remedy fails you, you are more likely to panic. this can quickly become a vicious cycle. stay calm soldier on.
OXYGEN!
high flow oxygen usually counters an attack in about 20 minutes if I catch it at the right time. since Ive accepted my diagnosis from a neurologist I was lucky enough to get oxygen prescribed to me from a doctor at a free clinic. I did this without any insurance and was able to find a local oxygen vendor who also deals with the general public.
MAGIC MUSHROOMS
these are last on the list for a reason. that reason mainly being their legal status. make no mistake these things will change your life. obviously not for kids and use them at your own discretion. that being said I have found incredible relief dosing with MM. sometimes short lived but still better than anything over the counter.

DMT is a very powerful endogenous hallucinogen. I don't recommend it as a first, or even second line of therapy for cluster headache. For those with a stubborn, chronic condition, this may be helpful.
vaporized DMT has some advantages over MM or L
-tolerance fades within an hour. This means that you can redose pretty much whenever, without fear of "shutting the door".
-The onset of effects is within seconds, making it a good abortive and takes at most 10-20 minutes to get back to baseline. The duration of treatment is more or less up to the user.
- You can dissolve DMT freebase in e-liquid and use it in a commercial off the shelf vape mod, typically used by people who vape nicotine. This provides a portable and socially inconspicuous way to abort a headache in an emergency without interfering with your busting success.
So first...
The DMT:
DMT may be extracted with relative ease from plant matter known to contain it. This includes Mimosa Hostilis root bark (MHRB) or Acacia Confusa root bark (ACRB). Here is a link to a good protocol.
https://wiki.dmt-nexus.me/Marsofold's_tek
As a side note, I would skip the washing step at the end. You risk losing a lot of product. Just decant as much of the recrystallization solvent as you can and let your product dry in front of a fan in a well ventilated space. Once the it appears totally dry, it isn't. You need to wait about a week until the smell of naptha is totally gone before proceeding to the next step.
The e-liquid:
You need to purchase USP food grade propylene glycol (PG) and USP food grade vegetable glycerin (VG). They are both available online from amazon.com. I like to use a ratio of 30% PG to 70% VG. If you are allergic to PG, use an all VG solution with a few drops of distilled water to lower the viscosity of the solution to allow proper wicking into the cotton of your atomizer. An effective concentration of DMT/mL is 100-300mg/mL, depending on personal preference. This will be high enough to achieve an effect, but low enough to prevent accidental transdimensional transport. When you add the DMT freebase to your e-liquid solution, it won't dissolve until you heat it. Just place your eliquid container under the hot water at the faucet. With some gentle shaking, it will go into solution in a few minutes.
The Vape -
Now that you have your liquid, pick out a good sub-ohm direct to lung (DTL) vape mod. The setup is often sold in pieces including the mod, the battery, and the tank. Stick to a tank that supports an atomizer around .3 ohm. This will give you vapor that is easy on the lungs, but hot enough to carry your product. I like this one because it is a kit that comes with everything you need, except extra atomizers
https://www.myvaporstore.com/Eleaf-iStick-Power-Nano-Kit-p/elis-ispwn10.htm
(Eleaf iStick Power Nano Kit) and is very small. 25 Watts of power seems ideal. Although many mods support up to 80W power.
Alternatively - You may vaporize DMT with the "sherlock style glass vapor genie". A bowl

Hello everyone,
TL:DR
I may have discovered the cause of cluster headaches and have a working treatment plan, when previously very little worked for me. I went from fully chronic to them being almost completely gone. I've intentionally brought them back a few times, and then re-suppressed them to make sure it wasn't just luck.
These things have a cumulative effect, so while no one thing works well, in tandem there should be significant improvement. If you are desperate for relief, please give this a try:
Try clomiphene citrate, even if you've tried it before
Stay hydrated, drink lots of water
Listen to music in the mornings, especially music that gives you chills
Take 5-HTP in the afternoons
Try to destress / relax some - if an attack is triggered, try to remain calm and focus on breathing
Even if you've tried it before, start the Vitamin D regimen with all the cofactors asap. With everything, be very careful there's little to no soy in it. Some Vit. K gels are mostly soy. On sunny days, you may want to take more magnesium than usual, as your body is producing more Vit. D.
Lightly exercise as you can or go for a walk outdoors.
Avoid alcohol & sugar (including chocolate)
Avoid epinephrine (if you need dental work, share this with the dentist and ask for something without epi)
Try to minimize any opioid use (including kratom) if you have any for breakthrough pain. The only exception being Tramadol, as it can be helpful.
After being on the clomiphene citrate for a week or so, start tapering kudzu if you take it, as this can interfere with the clomiphene.
Be especially cautious of these things if you have a storm front approaching, as only one additional trigger will likely bring the beast back full strength.

Emgality (galcanezumab) 120 mg injection has been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the preventive treatment of migraine in adults. The drug is available as a once-monthly, self-administered, subcutaneous injection. The recommended dose is 240 mg (two consecutive subcutaneous injections of 120 mg each) once as a loading dose, followed by monthly doses of 120 mg injected subcutaneously.
The US list price of Emgality is $575 once-monthly, or $6,900 annually.
The galcanezumab approval was supported by results of two Phase 3 six-month trials in patients with episodic migraine (EVOLVE-1 and EVOLVE-2) and one Phase 3 three-month trial in patients with chronic migraine (REGAIN). In episodic migraine, galcanezumab reduced migraine headache days (MHDs) by a mean 4.3-4.7 days/month compared to 2.3-2.8 days with placebo (p<0.001). In chronic migraine, galcanezumab reduced mean monthly MHDs by 4.8 days
compared to 2.7 days with placebo (p<0.001).
The FDA approval comes less than two weeks after the US approval of Ajovy (fremanezumab) for migraine prevention in adults.
1
Emgality is awaiting EU approval following the recent positive opinion from the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) for approval in migraine prophylaxis in adults who have at least four migraine days per month. The latter recommendation is in line with the EU approval already received by Aimovig (erenumab).

Please let me start by saying if the vitamin D3 protocol worked to prevent your CH then stopped working, it will work again.  There's always a reason why it stopped working, the trick is finding what caused you to fall out of remission and take action.  You're not alone.  This has happened to many CHers and it will happen again.
One of the main reasons this treatment protocol stops working is an allergic reaction.  Other reasons include infections, trauma, surgery and Rx medications that interfere with vitamin D3 metabolism.  For example, If you had an infection and your doctor prescribed an antibiotic, the antibiotic causes down stream problems. For starters, our GI tract contains friendly colonies of bacteria called the microbiome.  Antibiotics kill off these friendly colonies and that causes further problems.  The solution here is to start a course of Probiotics and continue until the bottle is empty.
Allergic reactions respond to Benadryl (Diphenhydramine HCL) at 25 mg four times a day, 1000 mg/day Quercetin and 1000 mg/day Turmeric (Curcumin).  All three act as antihistamines.
Increasing the vitamin D3 intake is usually the best course of action.  Unfortunately, the 25(OH)D3 serum concentration is a poor bioindicator of vitamin D3 efficacy.  The CH threshold is a 25(OH)D3 serum concentration, above which you're CH pain free and life is good.  If your present 25(OH)D3 is below this threshold, the CH beast jumps ugly.  The CH threshold can vary from a 25(OH)D3 serum concentration of 50 ng/mL to 250 ng/mL so it's not reliable.
The more accurate bioindicator of efficacy of this treatment protocol to prevent your CH is the serum PTH (Parathyroid Hormone) concentration.  We've found CHers tend to start responding to higher vitamin D3 doses as their PTH serum concentration is depressed down to the low normal range of its normal reference range, i.e., around 14 pg/mL.  The trick is to do this without going into hypercalcemia, too much serum calcium.  This requires frequent labs for serum calcium and PTH.  The actual 25(OH)D3 doesn't really matter except as a reference point.  The 5-Year chart of my labs for 25(OH)D3, calcium and PTH is a good example.
As you can see, my 25(OH)D3 is all over the chart from 117 ng/mL to 273 ng/mL last May.  I varied my vitamin D3 intake over the course of this chart to remain CH pain free.  Now look at the PTH serum concentration.  It has remained between 28 pg/mL down to 10 pg/mL.  You'll also see there's an inverse relationship between calcium and PTH serum concentrations.  This is a classic indication of normal calcium homeostasis, the builtin feed back bio-mechanism that keeps our serum calcium in a narrow range.

This is the first time I have posted here.   I have been visiting the clusterbusters site off an on for the past couple of years - but I have never felt compelled to post anything here until now.  Every time I read a post where someone describes what they are going through I can relate.  Some of the stories here are really sad - and I hope these people take the busters' advice because its all good and has helped me immensely.  I have been reduced to tears reading many of the stories because I know what it is like, and I have had help and I am finally making progress and I there might be some light at the end of this very dark tunnel I have been living in for years,
I have been dealing with CH now for 13 years.  For the first couple of years I was told that I had a a severe case of Sinusitis and that was what was causing my pain.  The doc would give me some Avamys or some other nasal cortosoid, some antibiotics and send me on my way.  This 'worked' for a while as my clusters only lasted for a couple of  weeks and I figured that the sinus infection had passed and I was fine again.  This happened two to three times a year.  No biggie.   The attacks became worse and less tolerable as time went on.   I started to suspect that I did not have sinusitis - even though I felt like I had been kicked in the face and my sinuses were plugged.  At the time I did not have a regular doctor and if I showed up at the hospital or urgent care facility suffering from a CH I was often dismissed as 'drug seeking' and sent home, because the time that any doctor decided to see me the CH had passed and I was feeling 'normal' again.  I did eventually find a doctor who diagnosed me with CH, but he did not know too much about it and treated it like a standard migraine.   Maxalt as an abortive and amytripaline as a preventative - that didn't work, but at least I had a name for what I was dealing with now.  10 years ago the internet was not what it is now.  Cluster Headaches were less known and there were virtually no options for people.  My CH was episodic for 8 years - then they starting getting worse.  The cycles got longer and more frequent, until I got to the point where I am now.   Chronic CH - 4-6 times a day for the past  five years.  I get my 'breaks' from time to time where I will experience a one or two days CH free and I have good days (1 or 2) and I have really bad days (6+ attacks per day).
About three years ago I was fortunate enough to be put in touch with the CHAMP (Calgary Headache Assessment and Management Program) in Calgary.   The team of docs there are nothing short of amazing.  I have had MRI's done, I got a prescription for oxygen (a bit of a struggle to get initially from GP - CHAMP said NO PROBLEM), I got a scrip of Sumitriptan (Immitrex) and they started trying out different preventative meds.   I tried Verapimil.   It didn't really help me, I kept going for my ECG's and all was well until I hit the effective dosage and then BAM the worst tension head

Hey everyone, I've been away for a while. My cycle petered out in February and I returned to my "baseline". Thanks again to everyone who supported me during the dark times.
My "baseline" isn't plain sailing by any means. For years I've been dealing with more or less daily chronic head/face pain. It ranges from a 1-4 most days, although maybe I'll get the odd day off here and there. This "baseline" pain is mainly on the left side of my head and tends to occur under my eye, in my upper teeth, in my ear, in my neck (with autonomic symptoms too). I will get more painful exacerbations (6-8) every so often (over endurance, in heat/humidity, when my sleep is disrupted) although those never quite seem to mirror the classical cluster attacks I get on the right side of my head - they can last 24 hours, maybe more, and always ramp up slowly rather than come out of nowhere.
A few weeks ago I started to have premonitory symptoms of another more typical right sided CH cycle. Wasn't expecting it as I've typically been cycling only in the winter over the last few years. Started getting right sided "shadows". I got back on the D3 regimen and upped my melatonin dosage. For a couple of weeks I didn't get any cluster attacks, and was able to get an occipital nerve block. I still haven't had any classical cluster attacks as of writing.
Over the course of these three weeks, my left sided pain has been worse. And it feels like my symptoms yo-yo from one side of my head to the other. On any given day, I might have pain switching sides multiple times. I can often notice it on both sides at the same time. Whilst I guess I should be grateful that I'm not experiencing full blown cluster attacks, I still find coping with almost constant pain very draining. I do wonder sometimes how I manage to get by, being a father to two young children, and holding down a senior, full-time job. Life is a real struggle, although I do get some weeks/months where things aren't too bad (regular, light/moderate exercise does seem to help outside of my classic cycles). Being in pain to some extent all the time sucks the enjoyment out of life.
My neurologist in the UK is one of the most respected in cluster headache and migraine. So he supposedly knows his stuff. He's insisted for years that my left sided pain is chronic migraine, but I'm beginning to get sort of fed up insisting that it's more likely to by CH related. He says I'm ECH with chronic migraine, but I just don't understand any longer how the left sided symptoms aren't CH, albeit essentially chronic "shadows" rather than meeting the diagnostic criteria for CCH. I suffered migraine all through my childhood and those symptoms were noticeably different to what I have now. My neurologist said this is because adding CH to the mix tends to morph migraine more in the direction of CH to some extent. Does it even really matter what it is? Should the focus just be on treating it rather than obsessing over the diagnosis?

First of all, sorry for this not being just a "busting story", wanted to write an update since been away from the board (oops it's forums nowadays) for so long. And wanted to do this somewhere in the Login Required section.

Hi all,
I haven't really posted much lately but I have been reading and responding where I thought I could help. There are so many more knowledgeable people here now that I find my 2 cents not needed much. However that changed this past week. This is not to replace busting, this is merely a method that is working for me so that I can detox and get back to my busting regime.
I went back into cycle on the first day of the fall and immediately began busting with RC seeds (my normal cycle was 6 to 18 months). I was able to keep the beast manageable through November with only 2 to 3 low to moderate hits per day and pretty much free throughout the night. Unfortunately my first hit of the day was occurring at at a most inconvenient time for my 2nd job. I am a Basketball referee on the HS level and with my season starting I could not afford to get an attack and have to leave the court. I turned to 50mg imitrex pills to get through the games, I found years ago the I could take a 50mg imitrex pill and get a few hours guaranteed relief. Of course  the rebounds started happening and on the days I was doing tournaments (you can do 6 games in a day) I was taking multiple doses of the imitrex. It became a nightmare, I couldn't bust because I couldn't go 5 days, and the rebounds were getting worse, I was going to have to give up a main source of my income so that I could stop the imitrex and get back to busting. I kept looking for alternative relief  to see if there was anyway i could continue to work and get off the imitrex but still be able to get back to busting. text:
Hello, I’ve been getting cluster headaches for probably 20 years now.  I only get them maybe once (for 1-2 months) every two years usually when the seasons change.  I usually get about 3 headaches a day during this period.
I had a doctor I saw in Florida and the main treatments was prednisone, verapamil, and then an abortive like sumatriptan.  They also prescribed high flow oxygen.   They also prescribed anti-convulsants but I never tried it..  I guess I was scared of the side effects.  Usually by the time I  get all the meds and regimen going, my cluster cycle ends.
Fast forward to now, I now live in Japan where it’s harder to get a high flow oxygen due to limited resources.  So, I have some meds I saved (from 2 years ago)..  I started 50mg prednisone, 80mg Verapamil per day, and that keeps the pain down but I still end up using about 50mg of Sumatriptan per day.   I have the 20mg nasal spray for emergencies as well.
My current problem is that I’ve been on 50mg of prednisone per day for over a month now and I hate taking prednisone.  My skin is also breaking out and I’m not sure if it’s due to the prednisone doing something to my body or if the breakout (heat rash or something) is from taking super hot showers to help with the pain of the cluster headache?
When I anticipate an attack, I usually take 40mg Verapamil, and 30mg of prednisone, then I go to the 24 hour gym (very painful drive usually at 3am) and I either run for an hour or do the stair stepper (with deep breathing exercise's) for an hour or until the pain is manageable.   I usually end up jumping in the shower several times as well because the hot water helps to distract the pain I guess.  During the attack, It feels like there’s a fog in my head and focusing and waking up will help with the pain.
When an attack happens, I feel a nerve running down the left side of my head/neck and into my upper back is cramping.  I can feel this nerve run from my upper left side back into my neck and then up into the left side of my cranium and that’s where the intense throbbing pain is.
As most of you know, REM sleep can trigger these headaches so I often wake up out of a super deep sleep with a headache and an intense urge to run and get oxygen into my body.  I also try stretching the muscles in my neck but I’m not sure if that helps or aggravates the headache.  It feels like if I sleep on the left side of my head, my chances for waking up with a headache are increased?
I just now started reading about the Vitamin D3 regimen and I’ve started taking the Vitamin D3, but still have to purchase the rest of the regimen.   I really feel like I must start tapering off of the prednisone, and I need to stop the sumatriptan due to the rebound effect.
I’m flying back to the US this weekend for four days because I have some shrooms I want to take to see if that will stop my cycle.  I’m desperate to stop this a cycle because it’s debilitating.
I’m posting this just to see if anyone has any good suggestions.  Si

Hey everyone. First of all let me say what an amazing bunch of people you are on this forum. As much as watching the news makes me shake my head in disbelief and think "what is this world coming to", this forum and website brings out the best in people, helping others when they need it most. For that i personally thank you all.
Background.
Now then, i've had episodics for nearly 20 years now, mild to severe, though i'm sure some of you would kill for an "easy beast" compared to what you have to go through on a daily basis. the first five years through puberty completely "cold turkey" as no one knew how to treat it (as well as my GP thinking i just wanted attention..) After that Imitrex has helped me through many painful moments and made my life more or less "normal." Ever since taking it however, my cluster has been extended, up to the point that my last cluster went on for 11 months! Now this could ofcourse be that my cluster is changing, maybe into a more chronic version. However i started to believe that the daily doses of Imitrex are to blame for the extension of my clusters. My last cluster ended in september 2015, shadows returned in may this year, with proper attacks starting in end of july. Looking back at my history it seems that as the clusters are extending, so do the remissions. Not quite sure what causes that though.
Current situation.
When the shadows stopped and the attacks started, my injections were at the ready, aborting my attacks as usual. However this time i did not want to just rely on Imitrex alone, so came here (as i always do when the clusters are active) to find alternative methods. So now i am the proud owner of a few oxygen tanks with a proper regulator, as well as a cluster mask imported from the US. I also started the D3 regimen august 10th. Both methods providing me relief beyond my wildest expectations. I have not needed any Imitrex since week 35, all thanks to this wonderful website! The attacks are not gone however, especially the last week becoming more and more painful, especially at times when i did not have access to my O2, which i can't take with me to work. Somehow i managed to get through them without taking Imitrex, but it was a close one.
My lack of use of Imitrex for the past month makes it the perfect time to try and bust the living crap out of this cluster, so without further ado, here is my battleplan.
Battleplan.
I decided it would be best to take time off work and stay home, so incase of an attack i can use the O2. I have a week off work, with  possible a second week if needs be.
No imitrex is taken for over a month, and i just stopped taking my daily 5mg maintenance dose of Amlodipine that my neurologist prescribed in 2015.
Come monday or tuesday i will receive 15 grams of fresh psilocybe mexicana in the mail. My plan is to divide that in half, taking the first half on monday/tuesday and the second half on saturday. Then see what happens!
Any advice or comments, positive or negative are very welco

Hello All,
Long time episodic sufferer (over 32 years, about 20 cycles) who's been getting by in recent cycles with Verapamil and O2. Thought I would try the D3 regimen instead of Verapamil, it has seemed to help in the early weeks along with O2, of course, but week 3 & 4 the intensity of the every 1-2 hour nightly headaches was picking up and I thought I would try busting.
Living in Bulgaria so my options are limited but I was able to get some dried MM. I took one of the tiniest mushrooms in the jar (no way to weigh them, was told they were strong) a week ago Sunday. I could certainly feel them, especially hour 2&3. I was glad at the time that I didn't take more since I read you don't really need strong doses to stop CH. Didn't notice much help though week 4 is always a nasty part of my typical cycle.
I read about waiting 5 days but I couldn't find any rational why other than maybe waiting 5 days after stopping other meds that might interfere. Since I'm not taking meds, only the D3 regimen, I got impatient and tried again on Wednesday, 3 days later. I thought I would try a slightly stonger dose so I took what I thought was 1 1/2  times the first dose. I felt no rush at all in this dose, actually got sleepy, went to bed early and was headache free all night.
Unfortunately the next 3 nights were my worst so far, especially Friday. It's not that unusual this time of my cycle but obviously I wondered if it was MM related.
In any case, I was losing my patience and decided to bust again Sunday afternoon, 4 days after my last. This time I took one fat mushroom, which I guess was at least double the original dose, chewed it for as long as I could before swallowing.
After 30 minutes it kicked in, at 45 minutes I was getting nervous that it was too much but it settled in after about an hour to a fairly intense buzz for about 4 hours. I certainly would not have wanted more buzz than I had.
Unfortunately, I had a headache about 2 hours in and needed to hit O2 but it quickly went away. Then, about 4 hours after that, I got hit hard with my nastiest CH this cycle, a Kip9. O2 didn't help, I was stumbling around moaning with eyes closed like a zombie, left eyes weeping liking mad, left sinus feeling like it was going to explode. I'm about 13 days from being able to Bust, so I thought I would start a journal of my experience. My goal is for it to help me, as I find writing theraputic, but also it may help others in the same way that I'm finding reading the posts and journals here so very helpful.
My brief backstory
31 Dec, 2006 at 21:50, I got a headache. It lasted until July 2007. Yes, 7 months, non-stop, headache.
I have had migraines all my life as they run in the family, so wasn't too shocked when that Jan & Feb was chocker-block with them. The clusters also started the first or second week of January 2007.
The first 2 or 3 years, the clusters lasted 30-45 minutes, sometimes an hour. They were also like clockwork. Frequently on a Tuesday, often at 19:00, or 19:40 (or both). But they could also be quite random when they struck. Year 3 and 4 they started getting shorter, 10, 15, 20 minutes on average. Sometimes mild, sometimes extreme. Always scary, always with side affects. That's how I know it's a cluster, and not an ice pick, or a migraine, or just a bad, short headache. Well, one of the ways.
This year, year 5, they've turned from episodic (where I had 2 weeks to a 2 month gap) to chronic.
Tracking the Demon
For the last 2 years, I have tracked every headache I've had, all the meds I've taken, etc. etc. I have daily chronic headaches 71% of the year, which works out to roughly 5 days out of 7. This year I've had 40 migraines, down from last year's 63. But my clusters have blasted through the roof this year. Last year I had a leasurly 63, this year (so far) 226.
I know this is no worse than many here, and my heart breaks when I read of what other's suffer with. But it is a measure of where I'm at today, and hopefully I'll have better stats to share in a year's time.
So, why am I here?
I've known about ClusterBusters for a long time, and the use of LSD and shrooms. But I always considered it extreme, a last resort. Then, the last few weeks, as my cluster count topped 200, I started fixing a date in my mind that if I couldn't find a way to improve things, I would end things. And suddenly, busting didn't seem so extreme.
My plans and intent
Today, I received over 200 RC seeds in the post. But I can't do anything with them until I am off of Topiramate, +5 days detox, which works out to about 16 Dec.
Also, I am going to start on the D3 Regimen just as soon as I'm able, but Topiramate is holding me back from that as well, as it seems if I take too much Vitamin D while on T, it turns my urine on fire. Which isn't fun, trust me.
Plus, if that isn't enough, I've arranged through a friend to get some shroom spores. So long-term, I have that to plan for as well. (and learn about)
So, hopefully, I now have all my bases covered, and 13 days to read everything I can read on RC seed and shrooms. I've learned so much already!
I want to thank everyone who has put such hard work into this site, and has already given me advice and hope.

# SIX-LOOP DIAGNOSTIC FRAMEWORK FOR CLUSTER HEADACHE
**A Personalized, Biomarker-Based Approach to Treatment**
Daniel Huddleston • 2026
---
## THE PROBLEM & THE SOLUTION: THE HOUSE FIRE ANALOGY
A cluster headache is up to six "fires" (loops) burning at once. Treating blind is like using the wrong extinguisher.
## THE PROTOCOL: TWO-DRAW BLOOD TEST
1. **BASELINE DRAW** (No Pain)
2. **CYCLE DRAW** (Active Pain)
3. **COMPARE & FIND THE "DELTA"**
4. **TREAT THE ACTIVE LOOPS**
Simple blood tests, ordered together, reveal which loops are running too hot.
---
## THE SIX LOOPS
### Loop 1: The Chain Reaction
**What it is & feels like:**
Nitric Oxide (NO) release triggers rapid spread. Feels like rapid-onset pain, escalates in seconds/minutes.
**Blood Test Markers:**
NO Metabolites (Nitrate/Nitrite)
**Targeted Treatments:**
High-flow oxygen, Triptans
---
### Loop 2: The Survival Shield
**What it is & feels like:**
Cell survival pathway activated, cells protect themselves. Feels like attacks return at same intensity daily.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Serum Tryptase
**Targeted Treatments:**
Prednisone (first round only)
---
### Loop 3: The Pain Relay
**What it is & feels like:**
CGRP protein carries pain signal, dilates vessels. Feels like intense pressure behind one eye, attacks chain together.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Serum CGRP
**Targeted Treatments:**
Emgality (galcanezumab)
---
### Loop 4: The Sensitivity Dial
**What it is & feels like:**
Histamine release sensitizes nerves and vessels. Feels like sinus congestion, fullness, allergy-like.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Plasma Histamine, Urine N-methylhistamine
**Targeted Treatments:**
Antihistamines, Mast cell stabilizers (e.g., ketotifen)
---
### Loop 5: The Stress Engine
**What it is & feels like:**
Hypothalamus releases stress hormone CRH. Feels like clockwork attacks, worse with stress, blood sugar spikes.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Cortisol, Fasting Glucose
**Targeted Treatments:**
Verapamil, Vitamin D3 regimen
---
### Loop 6: The Slow Burn
**What it is & feels like:**
Recruitment of inflammatory cells re-triggers mast cells. Feels like a bout that won't end, feeling "inflamed."
**Blood Test Markers:**
CRP, ESR, IL-6, TNF-alpha
**Targeted Treatments:**
Corticosteroids, Anti-inflammatory protocols
---
## WHY IT MATTERS
It is not "treatment resistance." The variation is measurable. The map connects tests to existing treatments.

This is some basic information that I’ve been wanting to put in writing for a while.  The topics here are
doctors and diagnoses
,
oxygen
,
the D3 regimen
,
pharma treatments
,
other possible strategies (or supplementary methods)
,
shadows
, and
busting basics
.
Busting (treating CH with psychedelics, potentially at sub-hallucinogenic levels) is the reason this site exists, and it is a highly effective, low-side-effect way to end CH cycles and possibly prevent future cycles. Everyone with CH should consider it.
At the end of this post
, I have attached the basic information about busting that appears on various pages in the blue heading, "New Users -- Read This First."
I know I’m leaving out a lot. This should not be considered as a substitute for more thorough exploration. Just a starting point. I am hoping that others will correct, clarify, and amplify what’s here. One way to learn more about any of these topics is to put a word or words into the search bar at the top right of any page, and be sure that “all content” is checked. There’s a ton and a half of information here.
Doctors and Diagnoses
Most general-practice physicians are clueless about CH. Not only do they generally misdiagnose it, they don’t seem to have the energy or compassion to look up proper treatments when there is a correct diagnosis. Neurologists may be slightly better, but usually not much. Your best bet for diagnosis/treatment is a headache center, particularly one associated with a university.
Of course, a doctor’s treatment options are generally limited to pharmaceuticals. More about pharma treatments later.
You should have an MRI to rule out possible other causes of your pain.
There are a few conditions that are CH “lookalikes” that should be kept in mind, particularly if standard CH treatments (or busting) aren’t working. Probably the most common of these are hemicranias (which you can look up). Hemicranias are treatable with a drug called indomethacin, which should be tried if CH treatments aren’t working. There are also conditions caused by veins in the brain, called “venous headaches,” and cervicogenic headaches, which begin in the neck but create severe head pain. But if you have CH symptoms, the greatest likelihood is that you have CH.
Oxygen
You should have oxygen from a tank/cylinder/cannister,
not
a concentrator that makes O2 from room air. You should have either a
non-rebreather
mask or the mask that is made for people with CH, the “Cluster O2 Kit,” which you can buy here:
http://www.clusterheadaches.com/ccp8/
.
The flow rate should be sufficient that the reservoir bag on your mask is always full when you are ready to inhale using a forceful breathing strategy. For some people, this is 15 liters per minute (lpm); for others it can be higher. Since medical O2 suppliers generally won’t provide a regulator that goes higher than 15 lpm, you might have to get your own regulator. More about regulators below.
You want to have at least two tanks: one large one for home

I've just completed dose 3 of my busting protocol and I have to say, as I knock on my head and my desk, this weekend has been the first in 5 weeks that I havent had any attacks and only one minor shadow experience that lasted for a little bit Saturday morning then cleared away. Sunday was back to normal bliss.
I'm being careful not to get too far ahead of schedule with my excitement - nervous to what may still be hiding in there - but I have to say, I feel different this Monday morning. I feel like the pressure is mostly gone, the shadow is not persistent, and my general mood and clarity of mind are returning to normal.
I was living my life up until June 9th around 10pm when my first attack struck. I was about to go to the ER, sure I was suffering an aneurysm or stroke or something. The attack passed right as my wife and I were packing up to go out into the dark night so, of course, I chalked it up to too much wine and went to bed. The next few weeks spiralled into a hell I was not prepared for.
Thankfully, this community exists - and makes a lot of noise! So much so that when I started GPT'ing my symptoms (after the ER and my GP both told me to go drink some water and take two aspirins) I found TACs, CB.org, Cluster Head aches, r/clusterheads and the likes, immediately. Finally a doc diagnosed me and set me up with a Neuro... but that's a 6 months wait until they can see me.
I just happened to have about a half oz of APEs sitting in a box from last month so i went for it, all in on the process. I researched and read everything I could. I was watching 2 hour long zoom recordings at 10pm, soaking up anything I could to find more info, more possible relief, more answers.
I'm not in the clear, and this story is only just beginning, but the impact this thing has had on my life is wild. I've quit drinking (26 days sober from daily drinking), gone full keto (lost 10lbs so far), exercising every day, and have implemented the D3 regimen, the intermittent fasting, and a host of other quality of life improvements.
I wouldnt wish this pain or disease on anyone - not even the worst of us out there - but in a way it's been my grace... an opportunity to assess what I was doing before and what I need to be doing next... and I really think I am becoming a better person because of it.

My preference is to use the Bio-Tech D3-50 50,000 IU water soluble vitamin D3 as a loading dose.
With your last 25(OH)D3 assay at 86 ng/mL, you're at the low end of the average CHer optimum response range (80 to 100 ng/mL).
As we gain roughly 10 ng/mL in serum 25(OH)D3 concentration for every 100,000 IU of oral vitamin D3 taken, I'd take 50,000 IU/day for five days then drop back to either 10,000 IU/day with the oil-based vitamin D3 liquid softgels or 50,000 IU/week taking one of the Bio-Tech D3-50 capsules as your maintenance dose.  The Bio-Tech D3-50 has a higher bioequivalence than the same dose of the vitamin D3 liquid softgels so you can get by with slightly less taking the D3-50.  The Bio-Tech D3-50 is also the least expensive form of vitamin D3 at 23 cents USD per capsule.
I'd also start the Antihistamine Full Monty.  As you didn't experience a CH pain free response with your 25(OH)D3 serum concentration at 86 ng/mL, it's a safe bet you're battling an immune system response to allergens.  When insulted by allergens, the immune system's Mast Cells degranulate (spit open and dump their contents) releasing large quantities of inflammatory mediators as illustrated in the following graphic.
Leading this pack of inflammatory mediators is Histamine.  Histamine to a CHer is like Kryptonite to Superman - Bad News as none of the standards of care preventatives like verapamil, MM or vitamin D3 will work.  That means you need to treat the immune system response to allergens.  This is where the Antihistamine Full Monty comes into play.

Hi Scott! Welcome to the Board. Glad that you found us!
Well, you pharmacist may know meds, but not CH. The front line med would be Verapamil, starting at about 240mg/day and working up. A Prednisone taper is usually given at the beginning for a week or so to get you some pain free time while the Verap builds up in your body Long term use of Prednisone is not good for your body and should be avoided. The Verap dose can go up to over 900mg/day for some. It needs to be monitored with dose increases. And Imitrex is given in shot form for the fastest abort for CH. Nasal beats the pills given for migraines, but is not as fast as the injection. Pills work too slow for CH and should only be given for migraines. As you have found out, you cannot get enough of it to treat all of your hits. And you can't take an injection or spray for every hit! Most will load up on Trex when out of cycle to be better prepared when they get one. It is hard on the heart and the daily use is limited to two in 24 hours. Insurance usually only approves 9 to 12 a month. Which does not begin to treat them all!!! There is a way to split the injections into 3 doses that helps a lot of people. The full dose is usually more than is needed, especially when gotten to quickly. That does up your number of hits that you can kill with it. However, Trex is a double-edged sword - it can cause rebound hits and you wind up with an escalating cycle of more and more hits.
Ch has a lovely ability to morph over time. Frequency of hits changes, dates change, etc. Sorry that you have had this happen. Nocturnal hits sort of suck big time. Often by the time you wake, it is way up there on the pain scale. You can try sleeping in a recliner with it partly reclined - keep your heart above your head. It may not prevent your hits, but you will wake sooner to deal with one. That can be a huge help!!
Another help is Energy Drinks or Shots. Caffeine helps a lot! Some feel the Taurine in the drinks makes them work better than coffee, but for those who can't take them, coffee is great too. I keep it cold in the fridge for the nights. Grab it on the way to the O2 tank and get a faster abort. By itself, with a bit of deep inhales and hard exhales of room air can sometimes kill a hit too.
Pure O2 at 15 to 25 LPM will be your best friend for aborts. Not a concentrator! You will use a non-rebreather mask, so no room air is inhaled. Basically you force out the CO2 with a hard exhale with a crunch at the end and then a big inhale, hold for a bit, and repeat. You dial your O2 up to support the breathing - don't want to wait for the bag on the mask to fill, you need it waiting for that inhale. When the pain is gone, then you do your 'post hit' breathing. Dial it down and breath normally for five minutes or so. This prevents an immediate return of the hit. If your insurance covers it, get it! If not, then we can get you set up with welding O2 to use. No doctor needed then.
